origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Brockelmann 1928:704 connects it with Syr. qat (qtt) 'fix'. ETHIOPIC: Te. qātata (in Reinisch) 'make a wager'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. qatet 'make a treaty'. See also qatawa.

meaning: (T) = Amh. asäggärä 1) 'catch fish with net or hook'; 2) 'alternate', since the Amharic translation is without reference it is difficult to know its precise meaning.
origin_explanation: since the Amharic translation is without reference it is difficult to know its precise meaning.

meaning: (T.Y) 'pay tribute, bet'; 'aqatawa 'exact payment, make pay tribute, impose taxes, fix a premium for those who wager, require a guarantee, (Gr.176) claim back'; taqatawa 'deposit mutual security, sue for an obligation that is not met, (L) make a wager'; qatot 'guarantee, security, pledge, wager, bail, pact, treaty, contract, alliance'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. qatā (qtw) 'be in service of someone', Sar. m-qtw-y 'one who is in service'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)qata 'bet' (v). Tna. qatot (from qatawa) (tā)qāta'äččä 'bet' (v). See also qatata, above.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. qate 'small measure of grain'.

meaning: (L) q°aṭṭa 'butt end of spear, spear with squared iron part, slender spear'.
origin_explanation: Dillmann 471 identifies it with g°aṭṭ, above.

meaning: (T) taq°aṭṭa'a 'be vexed, be furious, be angry, be excited, be irritated, be indignant, be grieved, be disheartened, be impatient, loathe, feel disgust, feel aversion, be exasperated, despair'; 'astaq°aṭṭa'a 'exasperate, vex, render impatient, provoke anger, provoke wrath, cause aversion, irritate'; q°aṭṭu 'indignant, vexed, angry, furious, irritable, wrathful'; q°aṭṭa'a 'displeasure, vexation, indignation, anger, wrath, aversion, loathing'; masṭaq°ṭa', masṭaq°aṭṭa'a (Gr.180,Lt) 'displeased, irritated, irritable, subject to indignation, cause of indignation'; taq°aṭṭa'i, see q°aṭṭu'; taq°ṭa't 'indignation, anger, wrath, aversion, loathing'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tā)q°aṭṭa'a 'be angry', Amh. (tā)q°aṭṭa, Arg. äqq°aṭṭa. The connection with Har. qay äša 'get angry' suggested by Cerulli 1936:265 is unlikely. The root is related to qataṭa II.

meaning: (K*Mk) 'make a mark in order that woven cloth be symmetrical, trim, shorten'; qatāb 'piece, segment'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. qataba 'cut off', qutb 'thorn', Mh. qatāyb 'pox', Heb. qeṭeb 'sting', Aram. qaṭab 'chop, cleave'; related to Heb. qaṣab 'cut off', Ar. qaḍaba. ETHIOPIC: Tna. qänṣābä 'take off'. Te. qänčäba 'be small by nature', qočäba 'tear off'. Amh. qāṭṭäbä 'trim, shorten, make a mark in order that woven cloth be symmetrical'. See also qnṭb I.

meaning: (K.Y) 'pick, cut (flowers, leaves), pluck, snap'; taqatafa (K*), passive; qatāfī (K*), act. part. of qatafa; qatuf (K*), pass. part. of qatafa; qāṭāf 'picked (flowers, leaves), snapped-off (leaves)'; qatfāt (K*) 'picking (n), plucking'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. qatafa 'pluck', Heb. qaṭap 'pluck off', Syr. qaṭap, Akk. qatāpu (with t by dissimilation) 'cut off, pluck off'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. qänṭāfä 'pluck off' (augmented by n), Amh. qāṭṭäfä 'pick flowers'; related to qataba (see above). See also qatafa II.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. qātäfä 'cheat, dupe, swindle', Amh. qāṭṭäfä; developed from qatafa I 'pick, snap off > snatch, cheat, blame'.

meaning: (T.M) qatana 'be thin, be fine, be lean, be subtle, (Y) be emaciated'; 'aqatana 'render fine, make thin, make slim, make lean'; qatīn (fem. qatṭān, pl. com. qatṭanī) 'fine, thin, subtle, delicate, transcendent'; qatṭanāt (with or without albās) 'fine linen, soft clothing'; qatān 'thinness, slenderness, little finger'; qatnat 'fineness, thinness, delicacy, subtlety, acuteness'; (qatnata labb 'gentleness of heart'); 'aqṭānī (K*), part. of 'aqatana; maqṭanī (K*), same'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. qṭn 'small', Mh. qōṭən 'thin', Heb. qāṭōn 'be small, be insignificant', Aram.Syr. qəṭan 'grow thin', Md. qōṭāna 'small'. For qṭn becoming qtn by dissimilation, cp. Akk. qaṭānu 'be small', and Ar. qaṭīn 'thin iron of a spear' (so also Barth 1893:36). ETHIOPIC: Tna. qāṭänä 'be thin'. Te. qāṭna, Amh. qāṭṭänä, Arg. qāṭṭänä, Gur. qāṭänä. See also *qatana II.

SEMITIC: Perhaps Ar. qaṭīna 'be bent', used mostly for the back, but could be applied to anything else, e.g. a hook. It could also be derived from qatana 'be thin', the thinness being applied to the hook; in this case Amh. aqqaṭṭänä 'angle, catch fish' would be a denominative of Amh. mäqāṭən 'fishhook'. It is also possible that the original meaning is that of the verb aqqaṭṭänä 'angle, catch fish', without cognates, and that Amh. mäqaṭən (G. mäqāṭən) is derived from the verb; cp. also Te. mäqaṭən 'fishing line'.
From Ar. qaṭṭīn 'servant', from qaṭana 'reside'. Ei-tan, HUCA 12-13 (1938), 68 identifies the Ar. root with Heb. qāṭān in kəlē haqqaṭṭān (Is.22:24) which

meaning: 'silk, silk garment, cotton, (T) silk worm, spider web';
origin_explanation: From Ar. qutn 'cotton' (of Indian or Egyptian origin; see Vollers, ZDMG 50[1896],633); also Soq. qitn 'silk', Syr. qǝṭṭānā 'cotton spinner, cotton seller'. For Indo-European (English 'cotton', and so on) coming from Ar. qutn, see Lokotsch no. 1272.

meaning: 'smash, crush, break to pieces, break open, burst asunder, shatter, bruise, pound, grind';
origin_explanation: SEMİTİC. qdd, qss, qtt: Ar. qadqada, qaddā 'break, crush', qatta, qasqasa 'cut', Heb. qāsaṣ, PbH. qitqet 'spread out by beating'; related to Heb. qaṭ (ktt) 'crush fine'. ETHIOPIC. Tna. qātqātā 'crush, pound', Te. qātqāta, Amh. qātqātā, ṭāṭqātā 'crush, pound, hammer'. Har. qātqātā, Gur. qātqātā. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. Bil. qatqat, Kam.Al. qatqaččo. See also qtqt II, and qasqasa.

meaning: q³tq³t, (T. reprint) q³tq³t, pass. part. of qatqata; also 'afflicted, terrified'
grinding (n), breaking, bruising, destruction, wreck, piece, slaughter, humility (that is, breaking down in spirit), sadness, affliction, calamity, disaster, contrition, shattered condition, breach';

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC. Tna. tānqātqātā 'tremble, quiver', Te. tānqātqātā, ṭāṭqātā 'shake', Amh. tānqātqātā, Gaf. anqātqātā, Arg. anqātqātā, Gur. anqātqātā, Har. qitqit bāya. The root qtqt II is to be identified with qtqt I 'pound, crush'; anqatqata would then mean 'be crushed, tremble'.
SEMİTİC. related to Heb. qāṭ (qwt) 'feel loathing', ETHIOPIC. Amh. asqātqātā 'have an aversion to something', Tna. qit bālā 'be disagreeable'.
see asqatqātē.

meaning: 'the initial stage of the Geez syllabary'
origin_explanation: from Amh. q³tǝr, qutr 'number', q³tǝr 'the initial stage of learning the syllabary', tämārā 'learn the syllabary'.

From Ar. qatrān, qitrān; also Soq. qatrān, qatran. Fraenkel 150 derives Ar. qatrān from reconstructed East Aramaic *qṭrn on the basis of attested etrānā. Zimmern 60 derives Syr. etrānā from Akk. etrānū; however, Akk. etrānū is attested in the recent Akkadian dictionaries. Note that Syr. qāṭrān is given in Payne Smith 3598. ETHIOPIC. Te.Tna.Amh. qatrān.

meaning: 'pelican, kind of water fowl, kind of hawk'
origin_explanation: from Gk. katarraktēs 'swooping down, a certain kind of sea-bird'. See also qəṭrāqṭəren, and qaṭāṭen.

T renders it with Amh. dombit 'kind of sparrow'. Since qəṭrāqṭəren is likewise rendered with Amh. dombit in a Sawāšǝw (see Dillmann 468), qəṭrāqṭəren is undoubtedly to be identified with qatarāqten (see above). Note that qəṭrāṭəren of M 194 is a misprint for qəṭrāqṭəren.
from Gk. kētos 'sea monster' (Hommel 1877:XXIX, 84).
see q³atata I.
q³atāṭi (L.Gr.423) 'disgusted, who disgusts, who repulses' (note q³atāṭi, with q³, as against the verb qaṭata, with q);
see q³atata III.

meaning: be slender, be thin, be stunted';
origin_explanation: q³atata 'make flexible, make slender, make thin, (Lt) bend down'; q³aṭṭ. (L) q³aṭṭā 'butt end of a spear, spear with squared iron part, slender spear'; q³aṭṭi, qotit 'slender, thin, (Lt) one of refined manners'; q³atat 'thinness, slenderness, (T) small finger'. SEMITIC. Heb. qāṭ 'small', qṭṭ (nif'al) 'be small' (Eitan 24), Aram. qaṭṭai 'make small'. ETHIOPIC. Amh. q³aṭṭāṭā 'make small heaps and there', and perhaps also qāčāččā 'be stunted underdeveloped'; related to Amh. q³aṭṭāṭā 'be short, not be well developed'. See also qatata I.
SEMİTİC. Ar. qaṭṭa 'cut'; related to Ar. qaṭa'a 'cut', Heb. qāṭa. ETHIOPIC. Amh. qāṭṭāṭā, q³aṭṭāṭā 'cut, shear', q³aṭṭāṭā 'break to pieces' (augmented by r), and qāṭa 'cut'.
q³aṭṭiṭ (Lt.K) 'large, bulky, thumb'; q³atqaf, qatat (T) 'thumb'. ETHIOPIC. Amh. qāṭṭāṭā 'become bloated (stomach), be swollen'. See also q³atata II, above.
ETHIOPIC. Amh. q³aṭāt.

meaning: Sid.Ka. qawo 'dry season', Som. qāwi 'time of harvest'.

origin_explanation: see qob.
ETHIOPIC. Tna. qawǝa, same.
in view of Amh. qawsa 'ram', qāwlā is probably a misprint for qāwsā.

meaning: 'stand, stay, be present, remain, rise, arise, be in charge of (e.g. a priest who is in charge of singing various prayers at night or in the daytime), be set up, be established, have effect, abide, persevere, be valid, last, endure, halt, stop, be stopped, cease, be barred';

causative; also 'establish, constitute, set up, set upright, place, erect, fix, raise up, put, lay, station, accomplish, grant (a prayer), perform, confirm, carry out (a project), ratify, stop (tr), cease, bring to a halt';
'oppose, rise (against), stand one against another, conflict (with), withstand, take a stand against, antagonize, quarrel, defend, resist, defy, stand up for';
act. part. of qoma; also 'keeper, stable, permanent, guardian, responsible, protector, patron, overseer, superintendent';
standing one, who is placed, one who is settled, fixed, that which is in use, (Lt) head, principal; (with 'am) excluded, shut out, isolated';
standing (n), size, state, stature, condition, position, situation, constitution, stability'; (with 'am) 'exclusion';
'harvest season from the end of September to the end of December'.
origin_explanation: T.Y. render it by Amh. bālg 'season of the small rains' (normally in May);

meaning: ETHIOPIC. Te. qawa 'time of harvest', that is, the end of the dry season, Tna. qāwǝi, Gaf. qawā 'dry season', Amh. qāwa 'pick fruit'; also in Cushitic:
'cause to vomit', taqayəma (L) taqe'a 'be vomited', qayəvi (K*), act. part. of qe'a; qayəyə 'vomit' (n); qi'at 'vomiting' (n);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. qā'a 'vomit', Soq. qe', Heb. Akk. qā'u, kā'u 'spit' (from West Semitic). ETHIOPIC: Te. qā'a 'vomit', Amh. qā'a (a reduplicated root); also in Cushitic: Sa. oq'u 'spit'.

meaning: 'be red', 'qeha 'make red', qayyəh, qayyih (fem. qayyāh, qayyāh) 'red, ruddy, fair (complexion)'; qayyāhe (K.Gr.178) 'redness', (Lt) red color', qihat 'redness'; qayyəhənnā 'redness'; qayyəhyəht, fem., (K*) qayəhāyəht 'reddish', 'qayyəhāyəha, 'qayyəhāyəha 'gleam red, reddish, make reddish';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. qyḥ 'red'. ETHIOPIC: Te. qāḥa 'be red', Tna. qāḥe, qeḥe. qāyyəḥ 'red', Te. qāyəḥ, Arg. qāyyəḥ. Har. qēḥ, Amh. qāyyəḥ, Gur. qāy. Amharic qālla 'be red' is considered by Praetorius 1879:48, 75 a 'Fehlschluss der Sprache', that is, the y of qāyy which goes back to qyḥ was considered a prepalatalized y from li, and the language reconstructed a verb qālla 'be red' (so also Cohen 1939:28).
perhaps a variant of qāyan (cp. the proper name qāyan, qāyal 'Cain'), and to be compared with Ar. qāyn 'metalworker', Md. qanaia.

meaning: 'take vengeance, harbor rancor, hold a grudge, be easily offended, harass, threaten'; 'astaqayyəma (K*), causative; qayyāmi (K*), act. part. of taqayyəma; qim 'revenge, rancor, vindictiveness, grudge, resentment'; qayyəme 'grudge, rancor, ill-feeling'; 'astaqayyāmi (Gr) 'vindictive, vengeful, (Sw. in D 458) magician'; taqayyāmi, same; taqayyāmi 'vindictive, vengeful, grudge bearer';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to nqm: Ar. naqama 'take vengeance', Heb. nāqam, Aram. nqam (see also Noeldekke 1910:180). ETHIOPIC: Tna. tāqayyāmā 'take vengeance', Amh. tāqayyāmā, Gur. atqeyyāmā 'offend'; Tna.Amh. Arg. qim 'vengeance, rancor'; passed into Cushitic: Kam. qimi-ta, Tem. qima.
from Ar. qā'im, qāyim 'curator, superintendent'; also Syr. qāyūmā 'superintendent, manager'.
the translation qāga afār given by T for qāynon perhaps refers to a specific country.
it is an Amharic form; for G., see qss.

phonetics: qays, qas (pl. q°əyas, q°əyās, yaq°yās, yaq°yāsi)
meaning: 'leg, shin, shinbone, thigh'; maqas (T), maqāyəs, pl. (D) 'instrument of torture to pierce the ribs, leg-irons, shackle, stirrup, goad';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection between G. q°əys and Akk. qimṣu 'knee, shin' (so Holma 137, but von Soden and CAD have kimṣu) suggested by Holma, is doubtful. Von Soden 478 likewise compares A. kimṣu, kimsu, kiṣṣu with G. q°əys, but kiṣṣu is derived from kimṣu by assimilation, and kimṣu, kimsu cannot be compared with q°əys. ETHIOPIC: Amh. māqayəs 'ox-goad' (from Geez). According to Praetorius 1879:44, G. q°əys is used euphemistically for Amh. qit where it means 'buttocks'.

meaning: 'small room, room in a house where various objects are kept hidden, alcove';

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. qəzəza 'cut, tear'; related to the root qṣṣ (see qaṣaya).

meaning: (D 1395,T.Y) 'be sad, be gloomy, be languishing, be pensive, be feeble, feel dizzy';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. qəzəz bālā, qazazəz bālā 'be sad'. Te. qāz gāzā 'become meager'. Amh. qəzzāzā 'look sad, look gloomy, feel dizzy', qəzz alā 'be pensive'. See also qəzzy.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. qāzəqāzā 'be cold'. Te. qāzəqāzā, Amh. qāzəqāzā 'be cold, be cool'.
For a similar formation (that is, the root augmented by y), see qlly, 'aqlalaya.
from Ar. qadafa, see qadafa, above.
from Ar. gazāl 'gazelle'; also in Tna. qazal 'gazelle', with a transferred meaning 'beautiful girl'.
variant of qādī, see above; qāzi reflects a Turkish pronunciation.

meaning: 'storeroom, sleeping room' < Gk. koitōn 'bedroom', also Syr. qāzāyā 'sleeping room, inner or private room', Aram. qīṭūnā (see Krauss 528).

origin_explanation: T renders qewenge by Amh. wanza 'Cordia africana'. Since, according to M, qewenge is used for building the table of an arch, as is the case of wanza which serves 'pour faire les portes, les fenêtres, des tabot, des sculptures, des couvertures pour les manuscripts' (Strelcyn 1973:232), the term qewenge was extended to the meaning 'church'. Geez qewenqe is perhaps to be identified with Syr. qānqā 'chan-cel of church', Neo-Syr. qānkī 'sanctuary', derived by Payne Smith 3666 from Gk. konkhe 'shell, niche for a statue'. Payne Smith, ib. also gives Ar. qankā-niyy 'custodian of a church'.
from Ar. qaws; also Amh. qāws.
from Amh. q°ayya.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. qāy, same.
